entries.form_section: Gt imperf. with t preformative typically spelled simply ttkl
entries.end_notes: Whenever one sees a triliteral verb with initial /t/, one suspects development somewhere from a T-stem of a fundamentally biliteral one. This common Aramaic verb probably derives from the identical and equally common Akkadian, which itself would appear to be a typical development from a T-stem of the common Semitic root wkl (i.e. Aramaic/Hebrew ykl, but "to trust" in Arabic) that otherwise appears in Akkadian as the very common noun waklu, aklu, "overseer". See also AIOA p. 106.

entries.end_notes: Fragmentary.

Since the normal opposition is between "living" (i.e., "fresh and flowing") water and tahmia water, we wonder as to the complete appropriateness of the commonly accepted rendering "turbid", for surely flowing water can be turbid, while neither stagnant water nor turbid water would qualify as "living" for the Mandaeans. For further discusssion and the view of others who would connect this term with thwm, thwmˀ n.m. "abyss" see Băncilǎ (2018) pp. 106ff. But given the regular alternation between tmh and thm and the regular semantic connection between "to be amazed" and "to stand still" in West Semitic , we see little reason to doubt our etymology and our primary definition.
